content.problemStatement:
Given a `N x N` integer matrix, find the no. of pairs of row and column which are equal.

For eg. in the following matrix:



Number of equal pairs of row and columns is: **2** (Row 2 and column 2 are equal. Similarly, row 3 and column 3 are also equal)


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer NNN, denoting the no. of rows and columns in the matrix.
Next NNN lines contains NNN space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output on a single line the no. of pairs of row and columns which are equal.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 100). Next N lines each contain N space‑separated integers (0 ≤ a[i][j] ≤ 1000) representing the matrix.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of pairs (i, j) such that the i‑th row and the j‑th column are identical.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Row 2 equals column 2, Row 3 equals column 2, and Row 2 equals column 3, giving three equal (row, column) pairs.
No row matches any column, so the answer is zero.
Row 1 matches column 1 and Row 2 matches column 2, producing two pairs.

solution.approach: For each test case we store every row as a key (e.g., a tuple or a comma‑separated string) in a hash map together with its frequency. Then we build each column in the same representation and look it up in the map; the stored frequency tells how many rows are equal to this column, which we add to the answer. This works because equality of two sequences is exactly the equality of their representations. The algorithm runs in O(N^2) time per test case (reading the matrix and constructing N rows and N columns) and uses O(N^2) memory for the matrix plus O(N) extra for the map.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) – the matrix itself plus O(N) for the hash map of rows.
